input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements, operating within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain for Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) testing and validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Data Monitoring service visualizes real-time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks via a user interface for reporting and analysis; Computation Offloading service transfers ACC system processing (lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands) from on-board hardware to external resources; Remote Management service allows operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands remotely. Target users include operators for system oversight and developers for troubleshooting during development.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: ROS2 for low-latency communication between physical and digital components, NVIDIA Jetpack for edge computing on the vehicle's hardware, Python and C++ for data processing and control algorithm implementation, MATLAB/Simulink for simulation and model-based design of ACC logic, and SQL databases for structured data storage and reporting.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for computation offloading between devices in safety-critical vehicular applications, as dictated by hardware limitations and industry standards.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system employs three distinct entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on separate hardware components. The Physical Twin Entity manages data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication, along with inherent system resources (compute, sensors, actuators) and automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking. The Digital Twin Entity handles data management and control mechanisms, while the User Entity contains the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents. This entity-based structure follows ISO 23247 RA with modifications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system architecture follows ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA), with software components mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as individual ROS2 packages. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes. The entities are structured into Physical Twin Entity (handling data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication), Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), and User Entity (3D Visualization and Control Panel), each deployed on separate hardware components. The Physical Twin Entity deviates from ISO 23247 RA by encompassing general automotive functionality (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) rather than being limited to resource-specific OME.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system utilizes Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking with DBSCAN and Kalman filter algorithms for object detection. Data Storage is implemented using a SQL database, and the Data Translation component handles data serialization and unit conversions as described in Section IV.C.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The lower-level chassis controls steering and vehicle speed via a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller. Actuation components include Motor and Steering Servo, with the Actuation module converting desired speed into motor voltage commands, as detailed in Table I and Section III.B.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera. Data collection encompasses ego vehicle speed and detected objects in proximity, as specified in Section III.B and Requirement R2.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from physical sensors (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor/steering actuation) is collected via Data Collecting FE and processed by Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking and Data Pre-Processing FE. The Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Communication between software components occurs over ROS2, while inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer for real-time data transmission.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (enable/disable ACC, modify desired speed) are initiated via the Control Panel User Interface FE in the User Entity. The ACC Controlling FE processes these commands to compute required speed, followed by the Data Translation component for serialization and unit conversion. The Actuation FE converts the desired speed into motor voltage commands for physical actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components uses ROS2 network, and inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi using UDP transport layer. Network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability mechanisms, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment uses edge hardware including NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer and NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna on the physical vehicle. Computation offloading is implemented between the Digital Twin and physical entities, with components distributed across different hardware platforms for scalability and real-time processing.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247:2021 standard, which includes four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture, Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. The reference architecture (Part 2) defines an entity-based structure comprising Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities (FEs) responsible for specific tasks such as data transformation into human-interpretable formats and user interaction interfaces.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

This is strongly supported by the text: 'the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system in a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology', and 'Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level chassis, and the autonomy elements'. The extraction is specific, technical, and well-grounded in the provided sources.

The extraction accurately captures the three specific services described in the text: Data Monitoring (UI for viewing state data, projected path, object tracks), Computation Offloading (ACC processing moved from on-board hardware), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling ACC, setting speed, emergency braking). It also correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers). This is directly supported by the text: 'Data Monitoring: Monitoring ACC-relevant data... user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports... display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', 'Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed...', and 'Remote Management: ... management and operation of ACC remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'. The extraction is detailed and comprehensive.

The extraction correctly lists the specific technologies (ROS2, NVIDIA Jetpack, Python, C++, SQL) and aligns them with their roles described in the text. The description of MATLAB/Simulink as 'simulation and model-based design' is slightly inferential given the text explicitly states its use for 'more complex systems, e.g., point cloud clustering', but the rest is highly accurate. Quotes: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8 ... and C++', 'Data Storage ... into a SQL database', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the specific temporal requirement of 100 ms for clock synchronization found in the source text. It correctly identifies the context of computation offloading and the justification based on hardware limitations and industry standards.

The extraction precisely describes the three entities (Physical, Digital, User) and their deployment on separate hardware. It correctly details the responsibilities of each entity, including specific automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering, and references the ISO 23247 RA framework.

The extraction provides a highly detailed technical description of the system architecture. It correctly maps the ISO 23247 RA to the specific implementation using ROS2 packages and nodes, details the structure of the three entities, and highlights the architectural deviation regarding the Physical Twin Entity's scope.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text describes the internal system architecture, communication between the physical twin, digital twin, and user entity (using ROS2 and Wi-Fi), and general industry trends, but does not contain details regarding integration with external enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or specific external data exchange protocols.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the details in Table I regarding Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking using DBSCAN and Kalman filters, as well as the SQL database implementation for Data Storage. It also correctly identifies the Data Translation component's specific functions (serialization, unit conversions) from Section IV.C. Quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database.', 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system.'

The description correctly identifies the lower-level chassis's role in steering and speed control, and the inclusion of the Vedder Electronic Speed Controller as specified in Section III.B. It accurately details the Actuation module's function of converting speed to voltage commands and lists the Motor and Steering Servo components found in Table I. Quotes: 'The lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed of the vehicle.', 'list of these autonomy components include... a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller.', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.'

The description precisely lists the hardware components (Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera) and the specific data collected (ego vehicle speed, detected objects) as described in Section III.B and Requirement R2. Quotes: 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'The DTS shall collect the following ACCrelevant data in real-time: - ego vehicle’s speed; ... - detected objects in the vehicle’s proximity.'

The 'Not in Document' description is accurate as the source text does not provide details on model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, or error detection mechanisms for the specific Digital Twin implementation. While the text mentions Digital Twins are generally 'high-fidelity' and lists latency requirements (R7), it does not discuss the specific validation or fidelity considerations defined in the characteristic description.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

It correctly describes the Data Translation component's functions ('serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions') and the communication protocols ('ROS2' for internal, 'Wi-Fi with UDP' for external). This is highly specific and well-supported by the text.

Excellent detail. Correctly traces the command flow from the 'Control Panel User Interface FE' to the 'ACC Controlling FE' and finally the 'Actuation FE'. Accurately describes the Actuation FE's function as converting 'desired speed into motor voltage commands' (Table I) and aligns with the remote management commands listed in R5.

The extraction correctly identifies the communication protocols mentioned in the text: 'ROS2 network' for software components and 'Wi-Fi using UDP transport layer' for inter-entity communication. It accurately notes the absence of further details like topology or security protocols in the document, aligning with the text's focus on high-level architecture.

The extraction accurately describes the edge hardware (Jetson Orin Nano, Jetson NX antenna), the computation offloading strategy (ACC system offloaded to DT), and the distribution of entities across different hardware platforms. It is strongly grounded in the provided sections regarding the physical twin and architecture. Quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna and NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer', 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
